### Source A 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2fbbec29-f7c8-45dd-a5e1-45fe9877397f)

### Source B
It is a paradox to brand a technological innovation as tainted goods by its very name. ‘Deepfake’ is a victim of its own capabilities. Negative connotations and recent incidents have pigeonholed this innovation in the taboo zone. The rise of deepfake technology has ushered in very interesting possibilities and challenges. This synthetic media, created through sophisticated artificial intelligence algorithms, has begun to infiltrate various sectors, raising intriguing questions about its potential impact on education and employability.

The dawn of deepfake technology introduces a realm of possibilities in education. Imagine medical students engaging in lifelike surgical simulations or language learners participating in authentic conversations. The potential for deepfake to revolutionise training scenarios is vast and could significantly enhance the educational experience. Beyond simulations, deepfake can transport students to historical events through realistic reenactments or facilitate virtual field trips, transcending the boundaries of traditional education. The immersive nature of deepfake content holds the promise of making learning more engaging and memorable.




### Source C 
However, while these potential abuses of the technology are real and concerning that doesn't mean we should turn a blind eye to the technology’s potential when using it responsibly, says Jaime Donally, a well-known immersive learning expert. 

“Typically, when we're hearing about it, it's in terms of the negative – impersonation and giving false claims,” Donally says. “But really, the technology has the power of bringing people from history alive through old images that we have using AI.” 

Donally, a former math teacher and instructional technologist, has written about how a type of deep fake technology called deep nostalgia technology that went viral in 2021 can allow students to form a stronger connection with the past and their personal family heritage. The technology, available on the MyHeritage app, allows images to uploaded that are then turned into short animations thanks to AI technology. 

Here are some of the ways in which teachers can utilize deep fake technology in the classroom utilizing the MyHeritage app.
Teachers have used the deep fake technology in the My Heritage app to bring historical figures such as Amelia Earhart and Albert Einstein to life. One teacher Donally has communicated with used an animation of Frederick Douglass (above) to help students connect with Douglass’ famous 1852 speech about the meaning of the Fourth of July to Black enslaved Americans. Another teacher has plans to use the app to have students interview a historical figure and create dialogue for them, then match the dialogue to the animation.

Donally herself has paired animations she's created with other types of immersive technology. “I layered it on top in augmented reality,” she says. “When you scan the photo of my grandfather, it all came to life. And it became something that was much more relevant to see in your real-world space.” 

With proper supervision, students can use the technology to animate images of family members or local historical figures, and can experiment with augmented reality (AR) in the process. 
“It makes you want to learn more,” Donally says of animations created using deep fake technology. “It drives you into kind of the history and understanding a bit more, and I think it also helps you identify who you are in that process.” 

### Source D 
Education platforms are harnessing deepfake technology to create AI tutors that provide customised support to students. Rather than a generic video lecture, each learner can get tailored instruction and feedback from a virtual tutor who speaks their language and adjusts to their level.

For example, Anthropic built Claude, an AI assistant designed specifically for education. Claude can answer students’ natural language questions, explain concepts clearly, and identify knowledge gaps.

Such AI tutors make learning more effective, accessible, and inclusive. Students feel like they have an expert guide helping them master new skills and material.

AI and deepfake technology have enormous potential to enhance workforce training and education in immersive new ways too.
As Drew Rose, CSO and founder of cybersecurity firm Living Security, explains, “educators can leverage deepfakes to create immersive learning experiences. For instance, a history lesson might feature a ‘guest appearance’ by a historical figure, or a science lesson might have a renowned scientist explaining complex concepts.”
Ivana Bartoletti, privacy and data protection expert at Wipro and author of An Artificial Revolution – On Power, Politics and AI envisions similar applications.

“Deepfake technologies could provide an easier and less expensive way to train and visualise,” she says.
“Students of medicine and nursing currently train with animatronic robots. They are expensive and require special control rooms. Generative AI and augmented or virtual reality headsets or practice rooms will be cheaper and allow for the generalisation, if not the gamification, of simulation.”

Medical students could gain experience diagnosing and treating simulated patients, while business students could practice high-stakes scenarios like negotiations without real-world consequences.
These immersive, gamified environments enabled by AI and deepfakes also have vast potential for corporate training.

Bartoletti notes, “A similar use case could be made for other types of learning that require risky and skill-based experiences. The Air Force uses AI as adversaries in flight simulators, and humans have not beaten the best AIs since 2015.

parts.content: With reference to the sources and your own knowledge, discuss whether the use of deepfakes in the educational sector is an incremental or transformational change.

### Possible Responses

- Definition of deepfakes and how they work $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explanation of incremental vs transformational change $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Argument for Incremental Change

- Deepfakes are an extension/evolution of existing technologies like photoshop, video editing etc. $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Simply enhancing/automating existing capabilities rather than introducing entirely new ones $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Argument for Transformational Change

- Potential to completely transform learning experiences and educational models $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Examples of transformational applications (e.g. personalized learning avatars, virtual reconstructions of historical events) $\blacksquare$ <marks>2 marks</marks>

#### Other Relevant Points

- Ethical concerns around deepfakes (e.g. consent, privacy, misinformation) $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Regulatory challenges and need for guidelines $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>
A well-balanced response considering both perspectives is expected. Credit should be given for well-substantiated examples and arguments from either side.
</note>

### Source A: Killer Robots: New UN Report Urges Treaty by 2026
The UN secretary-general has called for states to conclude a new international treaty by 2026 to prohibit weapons systems without human control or oversight and that cannot be used in compliance with international humanitarian law. The treaty should regulate all types of autonomous weapons systems. The report reflects 58 submissions from over 73 countries and 33 submissions from the International Committee of the Red Cross and civil society groups. The UN General Assembly is considered a venue for inclusive discussions on autonomous weapons systems, considering international peace and security concerns.

### Source B: Operation PEACE STORM: The Role of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles and Autonomous Weapons in Libya’s Offensive Strategy
On 27 March 2020, the Prime Minister of Libya, Faiez Serraj, announced the commencement of Operation PEACE STORM, which moved GNA-AF to the offensive along the coastal littoral. The combination of the Gabya-class frigates and Korkut short-range air defence systems provided a capability to place a mobile air defence bubble around GNA-AF ground units, which took Hafter Affiliated Forces (HAF) air assets out of the military equation. Libya classifies HAF as a terrorist rebel organization. The enhanced operational intelligence capability included Turkish-operated signal intelligence and the intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance provided by Bayraktar TB-2 and probably TAI Anka S unmanned combat aerial vehicles. This allowed for the development of an asymmetrical war of attrition designed to degrade HAF ground unit capability. The GNA-AF breakout of Tripoli was supported with Firtina T155 155 mm self-propelled guns and T-122 Sakarya multi-launch rocket systems firing extended range precision munitions against the mid-twentieth century main battle tanks and heavy artillery used by HAF. 

Logistics convoys and retreating HAF were subsequently hunted down and remotely engaged by the unmanned combat aerial vehicles or the lethal autonomous weapons systems such as the STM Kargu-2 (see annex 30) and other loitering munitions. The lethal autonomous weapons systems were programmed to attack targets without requiring data connectivity between the operator and the munition: in effect, a true ‘fire, forget and find’ capability. The unmanned combat aerial vehicles and the small drone intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance capability of HAF were neutralized by electronic jamming from the Koral electronic warfare system.

### Source C: The Story of America’s Very first Drone Strike 
In the autumn of 2001, however, the United States was unwilling to launch a full-scale land invasion in a region 7000 miles from home. Instead, a plan evolved to send into Afghanistan a small number of CIA agents and Special Forces in support of anti-Taliban militias, with the aid of the US Air Force. That first October night was a powerful display of coordination involving laser- guided munitions dropped from the air and Tomahawk cruise missiles launched from the sea. General Tommy Franks, who then led the US Central Command (CENTCOM), the military command overseeing operations in Afghanistan, wrote in his memoir American Soldier that the assault involved in total some 40,000 personnel, 393 aircraft, and 32 ships. But one aircraft did not feature at all in the Air Force’s complex planning: a tiny, CIA-controlled, propeller-driven spy drone, Predator tailfin number 3034 which had crept into Afghanistan some hours earlier. It now hangs suspended in the Smithsonian Air and Space Museum in Washington, D.C., its place in history assured. Yet its actions that first night of the war – in which numerous agencies in the vast US military-intelligence machine each played sharply contradictory roles – remain steeped in controversy. 

### Source D 
Human Rights Watch released a report stating that representatives from around 50 countries will meet in the summer of 2021 at the UN to discuss worldwide policy alignment on ‘killer robots’ or ‘lethal autonomous weapons systems’. In their report, Human Rights Watch expressed objections to delegating lethal force to machines without the presence of meaningful human control. Bonnie Docherty, senior arms research at Human Rights Watch said: ‘The fundamental moral, legal and security concerns raised by autonomous weapons systems warrant a strong and urgent response in the form of a new international treaty ... International law needs to be expanded to create new rules that ensure human control and accountability in the use of force.’ Human Rights Watch proposes a treaty that covers the use of all weapons that operate autonomously that includes limitations and restrictions such as banning the use of killer robots, with many claims reinforcing that meaningful human control must be involved in the selection and engagement of targets. It goes on to define the scope and prevalence of ‘meaningful human control’ to ensure that humans have access to the data, risks and potential impacts prior to authorizing an attack.

### The Role of Drones in Surveillance and Privacy Concerns
Drones are widely used for surveillance in law enforcement and border control. While they enhance monitoring capabilities and can improve public safety, drones also raise concerns about privacy, consent, and the potential misuse of surveillance technology in public and private spaces.

parts.content: Discuss the impact of drone technology on public surveillance and privacy, considering both the benefits for security and the ethical implications for individual privacy rights.
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of drone technology in surveillance, with minimal or general statements on its impact on security and privacy.
- Descriptive response lacking evaluation; only basic advantages and disadvantages of drone surveillance may be mentioned.
- Limited examples of drone applications in public surveillance; discussion is not well-supported by specific cases.

Example Points:

- "Drones can be useful for surveillance."
- "There are some privacy concerns when drones are used."

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear understanding of the benefits and drawbacks of drone use in surveillance, with some evaluation of its security advantages and privacy risks.
- Partial analysis on the impact of drones, covering some security benefits, such as law enforcement monitoring, alongside privacy concerns like intrusion in public or private spaces.
- Some examples provided to support points but may lack depth or balance.

Example Points:

- "Drones help law enforcement monitor large areas more effectively, which can improve public safety and quicken response times to incidents."
- "However, drones can infringe on personal privacy, as they may monitor individuals without consent, leading to feelings of being constantly watched."
- Examples: Mention of drones in urban law enforcement and events such as large gatherings; brief mention of privacy laws or lack thereof.

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- Thorough evaluation of drone technology's dual role in enhancing public security and its ethical challenges in safeguarding privacy rights.
- Balanced analysis that assesses both the potential for improved security outcomes and the risks to individual privacy and autonomy, covering issues like consent, data use, and possible misuse of surveillance power.
- Well-supported examples from current applications of drones, referencing specific case studies or legislation that highlights both the security benefits and the ethical concerns surrounding drones in surveillance.

Example Points:

Security Benefits: "Drones are valuable tools in enhancing public safety, particularly in areas like border control and large event surveillance, where they offer real-time data that can help prevent crime and improve emergency response. For example, during public events, drones allow law enforcement to monitor crowds efficiently, quickly identifying and responding to potential threats without placing officers in harm's way."

Privacy and Ethical Implications: "However, the constant monitoring capability of drones raises significant privacy concerns. Individuals may feel surveilled without consent, which infringes on personal privacy and can lead to a chilling effect on freedom of movement or expression. Ethical concerns arise when drones capture data in private spaces or record individuals in ways that feel invasive. The absence of strict regulations can lead to potential misuse, as demonstrated by cases where drones have intrusively monitored protests or private property."

Examples and Regulations: "Examples include U.S. border control, where drones have enhanced border surveillance but raised concerns about data retention and consent. GDPR in Europe sets standards for data protection, emphasizing the need for consent, which some argue should apply to drone surveillance."

Conclusion: "While drones offer substantial benefits for public security, maintaining a balance with individual privacy rights is crucial. Implementing clear regulations and transparency about data use and storage can help address ethical concerns, ensuring that drones are used responsibly and with respect for privacy."

### The Role of Firewalls in Network Security
Firewalls are critical for network security, acting as barriers between internal networks and external threats. They control incoming and outgoing traffic, protecting against unauthorized access and cyber attacks. However, configuring firewalls effectively can be challenging, especially in large organizations.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5b8dbedc-2d3a-4187-acb5-f7d90f2921b4)

parts.content: Evaluate the role of firewalls in securing organizational networks, considering their effectiveness and potential challenges in implementation.
parts.markscheme:
Firewalls play a crucial role in securing organizational networks by acting as a barrier between trusted and untrusted networks, controlling the flow of traffic based on predefined security rules. Their effectiveness and potential challenges in implementation can be evaluated as follows:

- Firewalls provide a first line of defense against unauthorized access and malicious attacks by:
  - Filtering incoming and outgoing network traffic based on IP addresses, ports, and protocols <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Blocking known malicious traffic and preventing unauthorized access to internal resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Enforcing security policies and access control lists (ACLs) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Effectiveness of firewalls:
  - They can effectively prevent many types of network-based attacks, such as distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, port scans, and unauthorized access attempts <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Firewalls can log and monitor network traffic, providing valuable information for security analysis and incident response <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - They can be configured to allow or deny specific types of traffic, providing granular control over network access <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Potential challenges in implementation:
  - Configuring and maintaining firewalls can be complex, especially in large organizations with diverse network architectures and security requirements <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Firewalls may not be effective against advanced threats, such as zero-day attacks, encrypted traffic, or insider threats <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Improper configuration or rule management can lead to security vulnerabilities or unintended network disruptions <marks>1 mark</marks>
  <commonMistake>Relying solely on firewalls as the primary security measure, neglecting other security controls like intrusion detection/prevention systems, antivirus software, and user awareness training</commonMistake> <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Discuss the importance of regularly updating firewall rules and software to address emerging threats and vulnerabilities</examTip> <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Candidates should demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of firewalls, their capabilities, limitations, and the need for a multi-layered security approach</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the challenges of implementing algorithmic transparency and accountability in criminal justice, particularly with “black box” algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
### Key Points

- Algorithmic transparency and accountability are important for ensuring fairness and due process in criminal justice systems
- "Black box" algorithms lack transparency, making it difficult to understand how decisions are made
- This can lead to biased or discriminatory outcomes, violating principles of justice

### Possible Arguments

- Lack of transparency means defendants/public cannot scrutinize algorithms for bias or errors <marks>1</marks>
- Opaque decision-making processes undermine due process and right to fair trial <marks>1</marks>
- Algorithms may perpetuate historical biases in data against minority groups <marks>1</marks>
- Proprietary algorithms used by private companies are not open to public audit <marks>1</marks>
- Difficulty in assigning accountability when errors/biases occur in complex systems <marks>1</marks>

### Counterarguments

- Algorithms can be more objective/consistent than human decision-making <marks>1</marks>
- Some privacy/security is needed to prevent gaming of algorithms <marks>1</marks>
- Transparency could reveal sensitive information about individuals <marks>1</marks>
- Auditing/testing can validate algorithms without full transparency <marks>1</marks>

### Potential Solutions

- Regulatory oversight and auditing requirements for algorithms <marks>1</marks>
- Open-sourcing algorithms for public scrutiny and accountability <marks>1</marks>
- Human oversight/ability to override algorithmic decisions <marks>1</marks>
- Ensuring representative and unbiased training data <marks>1</marks>

<note>Credit all relevant points from both sides. Maximum 12 marks.</note>

questions.specification:
**Fake news**

We see and hear news every day and trust that the information provided is accurate. That belief may soon end.

Artificial intelligence (AI) software is now being developed that can produce fake video footage of public figures using recordings of their own voices. Using as little as one minute of user-generated content (data), it can reproduce a particular person’s voice. The developer of this software demonstrated the results by using the voices of Bill Clinton, George Bush and Barack Obama in a computer-generated conversation.

Once a person’s voice has been reproduced, a fake video can be created by processing hundreds of videos of the person’s face. Video footage of politicians are often used, as there is so much data available online.

Law professor John Silverman commented that, as humans we tend to believe what we see, and the increased number of tools to make fake media that is unrecognizable from real media is going to prove a major challenge in the future.

parts.content: Discuss the claim that companies who develop software that can create fake videos of politicians should be accountable for the fake videos posted by users of their software on social media platforms.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Software companies should be accountable (claim)**

*   Although there may be no legal requirement for the software company to monitor the videos of users of their software, there may be ethical reasons why they should, and it is not appropriate to hide behind an end-user agreement when the software has the potential to develop fake videos (ethics, values, transparency).
*   The software company has not been seen to take all avoidable steps to prevent the fake videos being posted / the software company can be proved to be acting outside the spirit of the law or maliciously (ethics, values).
*   The software company has allowed users to purchase the software in countries where there may be rigorous censorship laws appreciating that its use may be seen as unlawful (ethics, values).

**Software companies should not be accountable (counter-claim)**

*   If the software company has positioned itself as a responsible developer, and their policy documentation explicitly shows that they are practising what they preach (values, ethics) and have acted responsibly by minimizing the potential harm that may be caused.
*   It is unrealistic and unenforceable, however well intentioned, for the software company to be accountable for the content of the videos.
*   If the end-user agreement stated explicitly that the user would be accountable, would that clause be enforceable by the software company?
*   It is hard to determine at what point the software company would be accountable, as the software itself does not have the capability to cause harm, it is the user whodoes so (values).
*   At what point is a video considered fake? Is a spoof video a fake video (media authenticity)?
*   What happened to free speech or freedom of expression (values, ethics)?

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** politics, political speech, lobbying, machine learning, speech recognition, image analysis, deepfakes, media authenticity, synthetic digital media, monitoring, accountability, responsibility, change, expression, power, values, ethics

parts.content: Evaluate the extent to which AI-driven content personalization influences political power dynamics, considering both the benefits of increased voter engagement and the risks of biased information and manipulation.
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of AI's role in content personalization and its influence on public opinion.
- Limited evaluation of AI's influence on political power dynamics, with a predominantly descriptive approach.
- Few or no examples provided to substantiate points, with minimal reference to both benefits and risks.

Example Points:

- "AI algorithms personalize content, which can help people see more relevant political information."
- "However, this might lead to biased information that can influence people's views unfairly."

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear understanding of how AI shapes political content on social media, with reference to both increased voter engagement and some risks (e.g., spread of biased information).
- Partial evaluation with an attempt to analyze the influence on political power dynamics, though analysis may lack depth or balance.
- Some examples provided to support points, with a general discussion on AI's role in engaging voters versus the risks of manipulation and misinformation.

Example Points:

- "AI algorithms increase engagement by tailoring content to users' interests, which can inform more people about political issues and potentially encourage greater voter turnout."
- "However, AI can create filter bubbles, isolating users within biased information silos and potentially allowing political manipulation by tech companies or political advertisers."
- Example: Platforms like Facebook and Twitter use AI to promote political content that aligns with a user's previous interactions, which can reinforce pre-existing biases and create echo chambers.

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- Comprehensive evaluation of AI-driven personalization's influence on political power dynamics, with a balanced analysis of both increased engagement and critical risks like bias and manipulation.
- In-depth analysis of how AI consolidates power among tech companies, impacts voter behavior, and shapes public opinion by creating tailored information ecosystems. Detailed consideration of the ethical and democratic implications of such power.
- Well-chosen examples and insights into the consequences of AI-driven influence on political discourse and trust, and a nuanced view of how personalization might enhance democratic engagement but also risks undermining informed decision-making.

Example Points:

- "AI-driven personalization can boost civic engagement by showing users content that aligns with their interests and values, potentially mobilizing voters who might otherwise feel disengaged. Targeted content on issues like climate change or healthcare can empower users with relevant information, encouraging democratic participation."

Risks: "However, the personalization of political content also centralizes significant power in tech companies, which can manipulate public opinion by controlling what content users see. This influence may lead to echo chambers and filter bubbles, increasing polarization and limiting exposure to diverse perspectives, which are essential for informed decision-making."

Examples: "For instance, during election seasons, AI algorithms have been shown to promote certain narratives that align with users' beliefs, as seen with controversies around biased content on Facebook. The Cambridge Analytica case exemplifies the risk of misuse, where targeted ads influenced voter sentiment through hyper-personalized data points."

Conclusion: "While AI-driven personalization can encourage voter engagement, its potential to amplify bias and distort political discourse indicates the need for transparency and regulation. Balancing algorithmic benefits with ethical safeguards is essential for fostering a healthy democracy."

questions.specification:
**PRE-RELEASED STATEMENT**


Refugee access to services: governance, rights and digital identification. This pre-released statement is provided to support planning for extended inquiries into the impacts of digital systems on governance and human rights. It is intended to help you identify relevant concepts, stakeholders and examples you may use in the examination. Refugees and asylum seekers often need to prove identity or eligibility to access basic services such as food support, healthcare, education, shelter, legal aid and cash assistance. In many contexts, documentation may be missing, expired, damaged or inconsistent across agencies. These barriers can intensify discrimination and exclusion, particularly for people who face language obstacles, frequent mobility, low digital literacy, disability, or heightened risks from surveillance (for example, individuals with sensitive protection needs). Digital systems can improve service delivery by reducing duplication, speeding up enrolment, and enabling agencies to coordinate. However, they can also create new risks: coercive “consent” where services depend on agreeing to data collection; function creep where data collected for aid is reused for enforcement; errors that are difficult to correct; and unequal outcomes when systems assume stable addresses, consistent names, or access to devices. Governance choices, such as data minimization, data-sharing rules, retention, and independent oversight, shape whether rights are protected in practice.

**Source 1**

A host-country consortium in your home country (government social services, two UN-affiliated agencies, and contracted NGOs) is piloting a digital ID for refugees to “reduce fraud and speed up service access.” Enrolment occurs at reception centres or mobile vans. Staff capture: full name(s) as stated, date of birth (or estimated), family links, country of origin, current camp/settlement, and a photograph. The system also requests fingerprints and an iris scan; the help text says biometrics “reduce duplicate registrations.” A phone number and email are optional, but the screen warns that without them “updates may not reach you.” 

At enrolment, a consent page offers three tick boxes: (1) create an ID record, (2) share data with listed service providers, (3) use anonymized data for “planning and evaluation.” Only the first box is required to proceed. A QR code is issued on a printed card and, if the person has a phone, inside an app. At service points (clinics, food distribution), staff scan the QR code and see a “service eligibility” status (green/amber/red) and any holds (for example, “re-verification needed”). The governance note states: access is role-based; audit logs are kept; data is stored in a cloud region selected by the consortium; and records are retained for 7 years after “case closure.” A correction request must be submitted through an approved NGO office.

**Source 2**


From the excerpt from an NGO protection officer’s field note: “People tell us they feel they must agree, because the queue is long and the staff are rushed. When a family’s status flips to ‘amber’, they may miss a distribution and then can’t get an explanation at the checkpoint. Translators are not always present, and names are often recorded differently across documents. The pilot could still help if it reduces repeated interviews and if error correction is fast, safe, and not tied to immigration enforcement.”


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3edcb042-db65-487a-a944-76dbb74a572f)

parts.content: Identify two features of the consent process shown in Source 1.
parts.markscheme:
**Identify (Total <marks>2 marks</marks>)**

**Pipeline stages**

* The consent page offers three tick boxes covering different aspects of consent, including ID creation, data sharing with service providers, and use of anonymized data for planning and evaluation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Only the first tick box, which creates an ID record, is required to proceed through the enrolment process. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Data sharing with listed service providers is presented as an optional separate consent choice. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Use of anonymized data for planning and evaluation is presented as an optional separate consent choice. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correctly described feature of the consent process shown in Source 1, up to a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The description must be brief and specific to the consent process features mentioned in Source 1. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Marking guidance**

<note>Acceptable answers must focus only on the consent process features explicitly described in Source 1. Vague or generalized statements about consent processes without reference to the specific details in Source 1 should not receive credit.</note>

**Sample answer**

The consent process in Source 1 features a page with three tick boxes that address different consent requirements, including creating an ID record, sharing data with service providers, and using anonymized data for planning and evaluation. Only the first tick box for creating an ID record is mandatory, while the other two options for data sharing and anonymized data use are optional and the person can choose whether to agree to them.

---

parts.content: Identify two types of personal data captured during enrolment in Source 1.
parts.markscheme:
**Identify (Total <marks>2 marks</marks>)**

**Key points**

* Full name(s) as stated by the individual. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Date of birth or estimated date of birth. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Family links or relationships. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Country of origin. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Current camp or settlement location. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Photograph. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Fingerprints. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Iris scan. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Phone number, which is optional. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Email address, which is optional. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correctly identified type of personal data captured during enrolment, up to a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>. 
* Data types must be explicitly mentioned in Source 1 as being captured during enrolment. 

**Marking guidance**

<note>Both required personal data fields and optional fields are acceptable answers. The markscheme does not distinguish between mandatory and optional data types for this question.</note>

**Sample answer**

Two types of personal data captured during enrolment are fingerprints and the person’s photograph. Other data collected includes full name, date of birth or estimated date of birth, family links, country of origin, and current camp or settlement location, with phone number and email address being optional.

parts.content: Explain how the digital ID pilot could both reduce and increase discrimination in refugees’ access to services. Use evidence from Source 1 and/or Source 2.
parts.markscheme:
**Explain (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**

**Answer points**

* The digital ID pilot could reduce discrimination by decreasing duplication and repeated interviews, as the shared ID can help prevent refugees from facing arbitrary barriers or inconsistent decisions across multiple agencies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The QR based eligibility status system at service points could reduce gatekeeper discretion and favoritism, leading to more consistent and fair eligibility decisions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Discrimination could increase due to language and translation barriers, as translators are not always present and rushed queues can lead to coerced or uninformed consent, disproportionately harming vulnerable groups. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Data errors and inconsistent name recording can lead to status flips or holds, such as amber status, causing refugees to miss distributions without receiving explanations, which can disproportionately affect people from naming systems that do not fit standard forms. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Digital and contact channel inequality arises because phone numbers and email are optional but carry warnings that updates may not reach people, meaning those without devices or connectivity may miss important updates and face more holds. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Biometric risks include potential failures for some people due to injury, disability, or worn fingerprints, which can create higher false rejection rates and discriminatory exclusion unless alternative authentication methods exist. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Function creep and enforcement risks mean that if data is reused for immigration enforcement, groups with sensitive protection needs face heightened harm, especially as error correction should not be tied to enforcement. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for explaining how the digital ID pilot could reduce discrimination. 
* Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for explaining how the digital ID pilot could increase discrimination. 
* Responses must use evidence from Source 1 and or Source 2 to support their explanations. 

**Marking guidance**

<note>Responses that only discuss reduction of discrimination or only discuss increase of discrimination can be awarded a maximum of <marks>3 marks</marks>. To reach the full <marks>6 marks</marks>, the response must address both how the system could reduce and how it could increase discrimination, supported by source evidence.</note>

**Sample answer**

The digital ID pilot could reduce discrimination by decreasing duplication and repeated interviews, as the shared ID would help refugees avoid arbitrary barriers and inconsistent decisions across different agencies. The QR based eligibility status system with green, amber, and red indicators could also reduce gatekeeper discretion and favoritism, ensuring more consistent eligibility decisions at service points. However, the pilot could increase discrimination in several ways. Language and translation barriers exist because translators are not always present, and rushed queues can lead to coerced or uninformed consent, disproportionately harming vulnerable groups. Data errors and inconsistent name recording can cause status changes to amber, making families miss distributions without receiving explanations at checkpoints. Digital inequality is also a concern, as people without phones or email may miss important updates. Biometric failures could further exclude individuals unless alternatives are provided.

---

parts.content: Evaluate the intervention in terms of feasibility and innovation.
parts.markscheme:
**Evaluate (Total <marks>8 marks</marks>)**

**Answer points**

* The pilot demonstrates operational feasibility strengths through enrolment at reception centres and mobile vans, which increases reach to refugee populations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The use of QR codes on printed cards supports feasibility in contexts where phone ownership is low, as refugees do not need a smartphone to access services. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Feasibility risks exist because biometric capture and cloud storage require significant equipment, connectivity, staff training, and ongoing maintenance, and weak implementation could increase errors and wrongful denials. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Governance feasibility is supported by role based access controls and audit logs, but the requirement that correction requests must be submitted only through approved NGO offices may create bottlenecks and reduce practical feasibility. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The seven year retention period after case closure increases compliance and management burden while extending risk exposure for personal data, creating feasibility challenges. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The intervention shows innovation potential through combining QR codes with an eligibility status system using green, amber, and red indicators, which enables faster service decisions and better inter agency coordination. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The innovation is limited because individual technology components such as QR codes and biometrics are established technologies, so the real innovation depends more on governance and rights design rather than technological novelty. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The evaluation must weigh both practical feasibility concerns and the extent of genuine innovation to reach a balanced judgment about the overall intervention. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Award up to <marks>4 marks</marks> for evaluating feasibility, considering operational, technical, and governance aspects.
* Award up to <marks>4 marks</marks> for evaluating innovation, considering novelty and usefulness of the approach.
* Responses should provide balanced evaluation that weighs both strengths and limitations. 

**Marking guidance**

<note>Responses that only evaluate feasibility or only evaluate innovation can be awarded a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>. To reach the full <marks>8 marks</marks>, the response must provide a balanced evaluation that addresses both feasibility and innovation, demonstrating understanding of how these aspects interact.</note>

**Sample answer**

The digital ID pilot shows mixed feasibility. Enrolment through reception centres and mobile vans improves reach, and QR codes on printed cards support use in low smartphone contexts. However, biometric capture and cloud storage require substantial resources and training, creating risks of errors and wrongful denial if implementation is weak. Governance arrangements such as role based access controls support feasibility, but limiting correction requests to approved NGO offices may reduce practical access, and long data retention increases management burden. In terms of innovation, combining QR codes with a colour coded eligibility status enables faster decisions and coordination. However, QR codes and biometrics are established technologies, meaning the innovation lies more in system design and governance than in new technology.

---

parts.content: Your government is concerned that the digital ID system may be hard to access and subject to abuse. Recommend an intervention for the digital ID system that will address these concerns.
parts.markscheme:
**Recommend (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**

**Answer points**

* Provide rapid, explainable status and correction pathways by giving on the spot explanations for amber or red status with written reason codes and establishing fast track correction processes, including mobile correction teams, rather than requiring correction only through NGO offices. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Ensure language access by mandating interpreter availability either in person or by phone at enrolment and at service points, and providing simplified consent forms in multiple languages. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Implement alternative authentication methods such as PIN codes, caseworker attestation, or secondary documents if biometrics fail, to avoid exclusion of individuals who cannot provide biometric data. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Establish a firewall against enforcement and function creep by creating explicit legal and technical separation so refugee service data cannot be accessed for immigration enforcement, with independent oversight and sanctions for misuse. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Implement data minimisation and retention reduction by collecting only necessary biometrics, shortening the retention period, defining deletion triggers, publishing the retention policy, and releasing audit results. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Enhance transparency and accountability by publishing audit log access procedures and enabling individuals to request a record of accesses and holds and appeal decisions safely. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The recommendation should address access concerns with specific, plausible mechanisms that directly relate to the problems identified. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The recommendation should include abuse prevention measures with specific governance and technical safeguards. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Justify recommendations by explaining how they address the stated concerns about access and abuse. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Discuss trade offs involved in the recommendations, such as between convenience and security, or between data collection and privacy protection. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Link recommendations to evidence from the sources, particularly the concerns raised in Source 2 about rushed queues, lack of translators, and error correction. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Connect recommendations to relevant knowledge about rights, consent, redress, oversight, and proportionality in data governance systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Award up to <marks>5 marks</marks> for addressing access concerns with specific, plausible mechanisms. 
* Award up to <marks>5 marks</marks> for addressing abuse prevention with specific governance and technical safeguards. 
* Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for providing justification, discussing trade offs, and linking to sources and own knowledge. 

**Marking guidance**

<note>Responses that only address access concerns or only address abuse prevention can be awarded a maximum of <marks>7 marks</marks>. To reach the full <marks>12 marks</marks>, the response must comprehensively address both access and abuse concerns, provide specific and plausible interventions, and justify them with reference to source evidence and relevant knowledge about rights and governance. General statements without specific mechanisms will not receive full credit.</note>

**Sample answer**

The government should introduce an intervention that improves access while preventing abuse. Rapid and explainable correction pathways should be created by giving immediate explanations for amber or red status and enabling fast track corrections through mobile teams rather than limiting corrections to NGO offices. Language access should be guaranteed through interpreters at enrolment and service points and by providing consent forms in multiple languages. Alternative authentication methods should be available when biometrics fail so that people with injuries or disabilities are not excluded. Abuse prevention requires a clear firewall that legally and technically separates refugee service data from immigration enforcement, supported by independent oversight and sanctions. Data minimisation and shorter retention periods would reduce risk, while transparency measures such as access logs and appeal mechanisms would strengthen accountability. These recommendations respond directly to the problems identified in the sources and balance the trade offs between efficient service delivery and protection of rights, privacy, and proportionality.

questions.specification:
**PRE-RELEASED STATEMENT**

Trust in elections depends on both the integrity of the process and the public’s ability to understand and verify that integrity. This pre-released statement supports you in planning extended inquiries for Paper 3 by guiding you to explore how digital voting systems may affect participation, representation, and confidence in electoral outcomes. Elections are high-stakes digital society systems: errors, exclusion, or perceived unfairness can reduce legitimacy and increase conflict. Governments may adopt digital tools to improve convenience, speed, and auditability. 

Yet election technologies can also introduce risks: cybersecurity threats, opaque technical claims, vendor lock-in, misinformation about how the system works, and unequal access for people with limited devices, connectivity, or digital skills. Accessibility is not only about having a smartphone or internet connection. It includes disability access, language support, availability of help without coercion, and the ability to vote privately. Even when a system is technically secure, confidence may be low if ordinary voters and independent observers cannot understand what protections exist or how audits detect manipulation. Conversely, a system that is easy to use but difficult to audit may weaken accountability. 

Governance choices matter: who oversees the system, what independent testing occurs, how disputes and recounts are handled, and how the system communicates uncertainty (for example, what happens during outages). In a pilot, it is important to define what “success” means, how harms will be detected, and whether the system should be paused, redesigned, or expanded.

**Source 1**

Election authority briefing excerpt (pilot scope and design):
* Pilot limited to municipal elections in two districts; voters can choose paper voting or the e-voting option.
* E-voting requires voter registration plus a one-time identity check at a municipal office or via a partnered post office.
* The authority claims the system uses a blockchain-based ledger to make recorded votes “tamper-evident”.
* Voters receive a digital receipt after voting and can later check that their vote record is included in the public audit dataset (without showing who they voted for, according to the authority).
* Accessibility measures listed: multilingual instructions, a helpline, and an assisted-voting option at supervised kiosks.
* Contingency plan: if network disruption occurs, kiosks switch to paper ballots; remote voting is paused until service resumes.

 **Source 2**
 
Excerpt from a civil society coalition statement:
* “A receipt can enable coercion if families or employers pressure voters to ‘prove’ participation, even if candidate choice is hidden.”
* “The identity check creates a new barrier for people with limited time, transport, or documentation, especially in informal housing.”
* “Most voters cannot evaluate blockchain claims; transparency must include clear explanations and independent audits, not marketing language.”
* “If only some districts pilot the system, unequal experiences could affect trust across the city.”

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/434c9a56-eefc-4965-b092-17fe2241e414)

parts.content: Analyse how the identity-check requirement described in Source 1 could affect participation and representation in the pilot districts.
parts.markscheme:
**Analyse (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**

**Pipeline stages / Key points**

* Requiring a one-time trip to a municipal office or post office can reduce turnout among people with limited time, transport, or documentation, lowering overall participation in pilot districts. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* If informal-housing residents or marginalised groups are less able to complete the identity check, their participation drops disproportionately, leading to under-representation of their political preferences in results. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Identity checks may increase confidence for some voters that the system prevents duplicate or fraudulent voting, potentially increasing willingness to use e-voting and participate among trust-sensitive groups. <marks>2 marks</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Award up to two marks per developed analytical point that explains cause and effect on participation and representation, to a maximum of six marks. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Marking guidance**

<note>
The response must refer to the identity-check requirement described in Source one and explain its implications using clear cause and effect reasoning. Higher marks should be awarded for analysis that explicitly addresses both participation and representation. Responses may also draw on relevant points from Source two but must focus on identity-check impacts.
</note>

**Sample answer**

The identity-check requirement affects participation by adding an extra step to the voting process. Requiring voters to make a one-time trip to a municipal office or post office can reduce turnout among people with limited time, transport, or official documentation, which lowers overall participation in the pilot districts.

The identity check also affects representation. If informal-housing residents or marginalised groups are less able to complete the verification step, their participation declines more than that of other voters, leading to their political preferences being under-represented in the results.

At the same time, identity checks may increase confidence among some voters that the system prevents duplicate or fraudulent voting. This increased trust could encourage participation among voters who are sensitive to concerns about electoral integrity.

parts.content: Evaluate the intervention in terms of ethics and cost.
parts.markscheme:
**Evaluate (Total <marks>8 marks</marks>)**

**Answer points**

* Coercion risk via receipts means that even if candidate choice is hidden, a receipt enabling proof of participation can facilitate coercion or undue influence. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Privacy and secrecy norms require that public audit dataset inclusion checks must avoid re-identification, and ethical acceptability depends on whether secrecy and confidentiality are robust and understood by voters. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Transparency ethics are compromised when blockchain claims function as marketing, and ethical governance requires independent audits and understandable explanations rather than technical jargon. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Accessibility and assisted voting through supervised kiosks can improve inclusion, but assisted voting can threaten privacy if not carefully designed to prevent observation of vote choices. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Direct financial costs include kiosks, helpline staffing, identity-check infrastructure, cybersecurity measures, audits, training, and ongoing maintenance of multiple system components. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Opportunity and administrative costs arise from running parallel paper and e-voting systems, increasing complexity, while identity checks add staffing and time requirements, and outages require contingency planning. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Vendor lock-in risk means that if the system is proprietary, long-term costs may rise because auditing, maintenance, and upgrades depend on the vendor. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Potential cost savings from reduced paper usage and faster counting must be weighed against substantial upfront investment and ongoing security and maintenance costs. <marks>2 marks</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Award two marks per developed evaluative point that explicitly links to ethical or cost considerations and weighs benefits against risks, to a maximum of eight marks. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Marking guidance**

<note>
The evaluation must consider both ethical issues and cost issues. Higher marks should be awarded for responses that weigh benefits and risks and reach a reasoned judgement. Responses should draw on the source material and may include relevant own knowledge about digital system ethics and costs.
</note>

**Sample answer**

The e-voting intervention raises several ethical issues that must be weighed against its potential benefits. One key concern is coercion risk from digital receipts. Even if vote choice remains secret, a receipt that proves participation could allow families or employers to pressure voters, undermining the principle of the secret ballot. Privacy and secrecy norms are also important. Although the public audit dataset allows voters to check inclusion, it must be designed to prevent any risk of re-identification, and voters must clearly understand what protections exist.

Transparency presents another ethical challenge. Claims about blockchain and tamper-evidence can function as marketing rather than meaningful accountability. Ethical governance requires independent audits and clear explanations that ordinary voters can understand. Accessibility measures such as supervised kiosks can improve inclusion, but assisted voting risks compromising privacy if helpers can observe vote choices.

The system also involves significant costs. Direct financial costs include kiosks, helpline staff, identity-check infrastructure, cybersecurity, audits, training, and maintenance. There are also administrative and opportunity costs from running paper and digital systems in parallel and from managing identity checks and contingencies. Vendor lock-in could raise long-term costs if the authority becomes dependent on a single provider. These costs must be balanced against possible benefits such as faster counting and reduced paper use.

parts.content: With reference to the proposed election pilot scheme for the locale herein and your own inquiries, recommend a redesign/improvement intervention for their elections which will address **two out of the four concerns** raised in the civil society coalition statement.
parts.markscheme:
**Recommend (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**

**Pipeline stages / Key points / Answer points**

* Make the receipt optional or redesign it as a non-transferable confirmation that cannot be used to prove participation to a coercer, such as a time-limited on-screen confirmation with no take-home option. <marks>3 marks</marks>
* Provide clear voter education explaining that the receipt checks inclusion only, not vote choice, and warn about coercion while offering reporting channels. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Expand identity-check options through mobile registration teams, extended hours, community verification, multiple document types, accessible locations, and support for informal-housing residents. <marks>3 marks</marks>
* Ensure paper voting remains fully accessible and well-resourced so identity checks do not become de facto compulsory. <marks>2 marks</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Clearly selects and targets two civil society concerns without drifting into unrelated issues. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Proposes high-quality and specific redesign features for the first concern. <marks>3 marks</marks>
* Proposes high-quality and specific redesign features for the second concern. <marks>3 marks</marks>
* Justifies trade-offs between security, privacy, accessibility, and auditability. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Integrates own inquiries and knowledge, such as coercion-resistance principles and accessibility standards, alongside source material. <marks>2 marks</marks>

**Marking guidance**

<note>
The response must address exactly two of the four concerns raised in Source two. To reach higher marks, the recommendation must explicitly link to Source one and Source two and integrate relevant own knowledge. Responses using only sources or only own knowledge are capped at <marks>8 marks</marks>.
</note>

**Sample answer**

To address the coercion risk from receipts and the identity-check barrier, two targeted redesigns are recommended. First, the digital receipt should be optional and redesigned as a non-transferable, on-screen confirmation that appears briefly after voting and cannot be saved, shared, or taken away. This allows voters to verify that their vote was recorded while preventing the receipt from being used as proof of participation for coercion. Clear voter education should explain that the receipt confirms inclusion only and does not reveal vote choice, and should include information on how to report coercion.

Second, the identity-check barrier should be reduced by expanding verification options. Mobile registration teams could visit community centres and informal housing areas, while extended evening and weekend hours would help voters with inflexible work schedules. Accepting multiple document types and allowing community verification would further reduce exclusion. At the same time, paper voting must remain fully accessible and well-resourced so that voters who cannot complete identity checks are not effectively excluded.

These recommendations balance security with accessibility and privacy. Optional, non-transferable receipts preserve auditability while reducing coercion risk, and expanded identity-check options maintain system integrity without disproportionately excluding marginalised voters.

Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>):

questions.specification:
**PRE-RELEASED STATEMENT**  


Platform governance: participation, representation and online harm in moderated spaces  

This pre-released statement is provided to support planning for extended inquiries into digital society issues connected to governance and human rights. It is intended to help you prepare concepts, examples and stakeholder perspectives for the examination.

Online platforms host political debate, cultural expression, and community organizing, but they also amplify harassment, misinformation, hate speech, and content that may incite harm. Moderation decisions can influence whose voices are heard and whose participation becomes risky or exhausting. When platforms operate across languages and contexts, a single policy can have uneven effects: slang, reclaimed terms, satire, and local politics can be misread, while coordinated abuse can evade detection.

Digital governance questions arise about due process (notification, reasons, appeals), transparency (what rules are applied and how consistently), and proportionality (whether burdens on users match the safety benefits delivered). Automated moderation tools may increase speed and scale, but errors can damage livelihoods for creators, silence marginalized groups, or allow harmful content to remain visible. Human review improves nuance but can be slow, inconsistent, and costly; reviewers may also face wellbeing risks from exposure to disturbing content. Effective approaches often combine technical design, policy communication, and accountable decision-making structures.


**Accompanying source booklet**  


Source 1: A short-video platform has introduced an automated content moderation system called “SafeStream.” Uploads are scanned in three stages:  
* text analysis of captions/comments  
* audio transcription and keyword matching  
* computer vision flags for violence, self-harm imagery, or extremist symbols  

The system assigns a “policy risk level” (low/medium/high). Low risk content posts immediately. Medium risk content posts but is “limited in recommendations.” High risk content is removed automatically, and the account receives a strike. The platform states that users can appeal removals and strikes; appeals are reviewed by a mix of contractors and in-house staff.

A transparency note says appeal outcomes are typically delivered within 72 hours, but “complex cases may take longer.” The platform reports recurring error patterns: false positives in minority dialects (due to transcription issues), misclassification of news footage as “graphic,” and inconsistent handling of reclaimed slurs. Users are notified with a category label (for example, “Hate speech” or “Harassment”) but are not shown the specific model signal that triggered the decision.

From a creator’s message shared with a journalist: “My video documenting harassment was removed for ‘bullying’ even though I blurred names. The appeal response was a template that didn’t tell me what to change. When my reach dropped due to ‘limited recommendations,’ I wasn’t even sure which post caused it. I want safer spaces, but the system feels like a black box, especially for creators who use dialect and political satire.”


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/bce92e11-8076-4af7-ab3f-3530a7535f69)

parts.content: Describe two stages of the moderation pipeline described in Source 1.
parts.markscheme:
**Describe (Total <marks>2 marks</marks>)**


**Pipeline stages**


* Text analysis examines captions and comments to identify policy-relevant language before content reaches viewers. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Audio transcription converts speech to machine-readable text, then checks for prohibited keywords. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Computer vision scans video frames for visual markers such as violence, self-harm imagery, or extremist symbols. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award one mark for each correctly described stage, up to a maximum of two marks.


**Marking guidance**


<note>If a response only names a stage without describing what happens, award zero marks for that stage.</note>


**Sample answer**


The SafeStream system processes content through multiple stages before it is shown to viewers.

It first analyses captions and comments to detect policy-relevant language, then transcribes audio into text to check for prohibited keywords, and finally scans video frames using computer vision to identify visual signals such as violence or extremist symbols.

parts.content: Identify two possible error patterns mentioned in Source 1.
parts.markscheme:
**Identify (Total <marks>2 marks</marks>)**


**Key points**


* False positives arise in minority dialects because transcription systems struggle to accurately capture non-standard speech patterns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* News footage containing conflict or violence is mislabelled as graphic content, leading to inappropriate removal. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Reclaimed slurs are handled inconsistently, sometimes resulting in takedowns despite being used contextually within communities. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award one mark for each correctly identified error pattern, up to a maximum of two marks.


**Marking guidance**


<note>The command term Identify requires selection rather than explanation. Responses must point to stated error patterns.</note>


**Sample answer**


The source identifies false positives affecting minority dialect speakers and misclassification of news footage as graphic content.

It also highlights inconsistent handling of reclaimed slurs used within communities.

parts.content: Explain how the platform’s moderation approach could affect participation and representation for different user communities. Use evidence from Source 1 and/or Source 2.
parts.markscheme:
**Explain (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Users speaking minority dialects experience false positives from transcription errors, leading to more removals and strikes that discourage posting. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Medium-risk content may still post but face limited recommendations, reducing visibility without clear notification about the trigger. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Documentation of harassment removed as bullying silences users sharing experiences of harm, altering which narratives reach audiences. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Journalists and activists posting news footage misclassified as graphic may self-censor, narrowing public information sources. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Inconsistent handling of reclaimed slurs penalises marginalised communities while coordinated abuse may evade detection. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Faster removal of harmful content can increase safety for targets, but false positives and opacity undermine trust and participation. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to two marks per explained impact where there is a clear mechanism and effect on participation or representation.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses should show cause-to-effect chains and address both participation and representation.</note>


**Sample answer**


The moderation system affects participation and representation unevenly across communities.

Minority dialect speakers face higher false positives from transcription errors, which leads to more removals and discourages posting. Medium-risk classifications can limit recommendations without clear explanations, reducing visibility for some creators. Misclassification of news footage discourages journalists and activists from sharing conflict documentation, while removal of harassment documentation silences marginalised experiences. Although faster removals can improve safety for targets of abuse, errors and lack of transparency reduce trust and willingness to engage.

parts.content: Evaluate the intervention in terms of equity and acceptability.
parts.markscheme:
**Evaluate (Total <marks>8 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Dialect transcription errors create disparate impacts for minority language speakers, reducing equity across groups. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Inconsistent treatment of reclaimed slurs penalises contextual vernacular use and reduces fairness for marginalised users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Misclassification of news footage as graphic harms equitable access to public discourse and journalism. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Generic notifications without specific signals prevent users from understanding or correcting issues, reducing legitimacy. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Appeals with template responses limit due process and procedural acceptability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Graduated actions offer proportionality but depend on user understanding to be acceptable. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Faster automated moderation can improve safety and willingness to participate for targets of harm. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Transparency gaps reduce trust and make the system feel like an unchallengeable black box. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to eight marks for evaluative points addressing both equity and acceptability with supporting evidence.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Evaluation requires judgement and balance, considering strengths and limitations.</note>


**Sample answer**


SafeStream delivers mixed results for equity and acceptability.

On equity, higher false positives for minority dialects and inconsistent handling of reclaimed slurs impose disproportionate burdens on marginalised communities. Misclassification of news footage also limits equitable participation in public discourse. Acceptability is weakened by opaque notifications and template appeal responses that undermine due process and legitimacy. While the graduated approach and faster removals provide proportionality and safety benefits, these gains depend on transparency and accuracy that are not consistently achieved.

parts.content: With reference to SafeStream and your own inquiries, recommend an intervention for the automated content moderation system that will most effectively ensure transparency and freedom of speech.
parts.markscheme:
**Recommend (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Provide actionable explanations with timestamps, excerpts, or specific reason codes to replace generic labels. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Notify users about limited recommendations, identify affected posts, duration, and steps to restore reach. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Replace template appeal responses with specific guidance and publish clear service-level targets. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Route high-risk flags from news, satire, or harm documentation to expedited human review. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Implement language-specific performance monitoring and fairness audits for minority dialects. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Publish transparency reports with error rates, appeal overturn rates, and recommendation limits. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Establish accountability structures ensuring platforms remain responsible for automated outcomes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Conduct human rights impact assessments to balance speech and harm prevention. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Provide accessible explanation systems in multiple languages on request. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Create an independent review mechanism for escalated cases after standard appeals. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Publish clear documentation with examples of what triggers each flag. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Establish transparency councils with diverse user representatives. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to six marks for specificity and feasibility, up to three marks for source-based justification, and up to three marks for integration of relevant course knowledge.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Recommendations should address transparency and freedom of speech while acknowledging trade-offs.</note>


**Sample answer**


A targeted transparency overhaul combined with context-aware review would address the documented problems.

Replacing generic labels with actionable explanations would allow users to understand and correct issues. Clear notifications for limited recommendations would restore predictability and trust. Appeals should provide specific guidance rather than templates, and high-risk flags for news or documentation should receive expedited human review to protect public-interest speech. Language-specific monitoring and fairness audits would reduce disparate impacts on minority dialect speakers. Publishing transparency reports and establishing independent review mechanisms would strengthen procedural justice. Together, these steps balance safety with freedom of expression by making moderation more contestable, proportionate, and equitable.

parts.content: Identify two ethical dilemmas associated with AI and describe their implications.
parts.markscheme:
### Ethical Dilemma 1: Bias and Discrimination

AI systems can perpetuate and amplify societal biases present in the training data, leading to unfair and discriminatory outcomes. This can result in:
- Biased decision-making in areas like hiring, lending, and criminal justice
- Reinforcing existing prejudices and stereotypes
- Disproportionately disadvantaging certain groups

### Ethical Dilemma 2: Privacy and Surveillance

The massive data collection and processing capabilities of AI raise privacy concerns:
- Potential for unauthorized access, misuse, or exploitation of personal data
- Erosion of individual privacy through pervasive surveillance and monitoring
- Lack of transparency and control over how personal data is used

### Ethical Dilemma 3: Accountability and Transparency

AI systems can be opaque "black boxes," making it difficult to understand their decision-making processes:
- Lack of accountability for errors or harmful outcomes
- Difficulty in assigning responsibility and liability
- Challenges in ensuring fairness, explainability, and due process

### Ethical Dilemma 4: Automation and Job Displacement

The increasing automation enabled by AI could lead to significant job losses and economic disruption:
- Potential for mass unemployment and income inequality
- Challenges in retraining and reskilling the workforce
- Societal and economic impacts on individuals and communities

<marks>2 marks for identifying and describing any two ethical dilemmas associated with AI</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the ethical challenges of automation in AI, particularly regarding job displacement and economic impacts.

### Key Points
- Automation and AI can lead to job displacement and economic disruption <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Potential negative economic impacts <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Ensuring a fair transition and equitable distribution of benefits <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Explain two limitations of copyright laws
parts.markscheme:
Limitations of copyright laws:

- Copyright laws only protect the expression of ideas, not the ideas themselves. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Anyone can use the underlying ideas, concepts, or principles expressed in a copyrighted work.

- Copyright protection is limited in duration. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright eventually expires, and works enter the public domain, allowing free use.
  - Example: In most countries, copyright lasts for the life of the author plus 50-70 years.

- Fair use/fair dealing exceptions allow limited use of copyrighted works without permission. <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Examples: Criticism, commentary, news reporting, research, teaching, etc.
  - The amount used must be reasonable and not harm the market for the work.

- Copyright laws have limitations on enforcement across borders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright is territorial, and laws vary between countries.
  - Enforcing copyright internationally can be challenging.

<note>Award 1 mark for each well-explained limitation, up to a maximum of 6 marks.</note>

parts.content: The evolution of the internet and computing has made it easier for people to illegally copy and distribute works of art. Discuss the impact this is having on the music industry.
parts.markscheme:
Some points could include: 1. Loss of Revenue: Illegal downloading and sharing of music files lead to a reduction in revenue for artists, record labels, and other stakeholders.
With easier access to pirated music, consumers may choose not to pay for songs or albums, directly affecting sales and reducing income for the music industry. 2. Challenges for Emerging Artists: New artists face difficulties in establishing themselves due to the loss of potential revenue streams.
They rely on album sales and streaming royalties to fund their careers, and piracy undermines these opportunities, making it harder to sustain a livelihood solely through music. 3. Shift in Business Models: The industry has had to adapt, with a shift toward streaming services (e.g., Spotify, Apple Music) as a way to counteract piracy.
While these services help reduce illegal downloads, they often provide lower per-stream revenue for artists compared to traditional album sales. 4. Impact on Creativity and Production Quality: Lower revenue may mean fewer resources for high-quality production, as smaller labels and independent artists have less to invest in their music.
This can limit creative exploration and reduce the overall quality and variety of music available to consumers. 5. Increased Reliance on Live Performances and Merchandise: To counterbalance losses from album sales, artists increasingly rely on live performances, tours, and merchandise as primary revenue sources.
However, live performances are not feasible for all artists, especially for those with physical or logistical limitations, meaning they are more severely impacted by illegal distribution. 6. Legal and Ethical Concerns: Piracy raises ethical issues, as it undermines artists' rights to their own work.
The music industry has implemented stricter copyright laws and taken legal action against file-sharing platforms, but enforcement remains challenging due to the global nature of the internet. 7. Potential Positive Exposure: On a positive note, free access to music can increase exposure for some artists, allowing them to reach a wider audience and potentially gain fans who may later pay for concert tickets or official merchandise.
However, this benefit is uncertain and varies widely depending on the artist's popularity and audience. Conclusion: The illegal copying and distribution of music have forced the industry to adapt, with mixed results. While streaming and alternative revenue sources provide some compensation, piracy continues to pose financial and ethical challenges, particularly for smaller artists. The industry must balance between protecting intellectual property and embracing new digital business models that meet changing consumer behaviors. 8 marks Valid points should be accepted with 1 mark for the point and an additional point for a supporting explanation/example.
